name: Sand (Fine grain)
visual_effect: Sharp golden nodal lines on metallic plate surface
best_for: General purpose Chladni demonstrations, educational settings, photography
behavior_description: Classic Chladni material. Grains are heavy enough to be stable at rest but light enough to be launched off antinodal regions at moderate plate amplitudes. Accumulates cleanly at nodal lines in 5-15 seconds. Shows moderate-to-high detail level.
limitations: High humidity causes clumping. Does not respond well above 1500 Hz. Too heavy for very thin plates or low-amplitude setups.
preparation: Sift through 200-mesh sieve to remove oversized grains. Dry in oven at 100°C for 30 minutes before use.
safety: Low hazard. Avoid inhalation. Keep dry.
availability: Craft stores, garden centers, scientific suppliers

name: Sand (Coarse grain)
visual_effect: Coarse golden or gray nodal ridges — individual grains visible
best_for: Low frequency demonstrations, bass-range patterns, outdoor displays
behavior_description: Requires higher amplitude than fine sand due to greater particle inertia. Patterns form more slowly (10-30 seconds). Nodal lines are thick and rough but clearly visible from a distance. Ideal for demonstrations below 200 Hz.
limitations: Poor definition at high frequencies. Requires high-amplitude drivers. Pattern edges are blurry due to particle size.
preparation: Use as-is from clean beach sand or purchase play sand. Remove debris.
availability: Hardware stores, home improvement centers, toy stores

name: Lycopodium Powder
visual_effect: Fine yellowish-green dust traces — extremely sharp pattern edges, near-holographic clarity
best_for: High-frequency patterns, fragile plates (glass, acrylic), precise academic study, macro photography
behavior_description: Lycopodium spores (club moss, Lycopodium clavatum) are the most sensitive dry powder for Chladni demonstrations. Responds at amplitudes 10-50× lower than sand. Forms extremely sharp nodal lines with single-particle-layer thickness. Hydrophobic — does not clump with humidity.
limitations: Very light — any air current disrupts the pattern instantly. Requires enclosed or draft-free environment. COMBUSTION HAZARD — do not use near flames. Expensive compared to sand.
preparation: No preparation needed. Store in sealed container. Keep dry.
safety: Fine dust inhalation hazard — wear dust mask. HIGHLY COMBUSTIBLE as airborne dust cloud — fire hazard. Never blow near flames.
availability: Scientific supply companies, pharmacy (traditional use in medicine)

name: Sodium Chloride (Table Salt)
visual_effect: Brilliant white crystalline lines against metallic plate surface — high contrast
best_for: Photography (maximum visual contrast), educational demonstrations, any setting where high-visibility is needed
behavior_description: Table salt (NaCl) produces the most visually striking Chladni patterns due to its bright white cubic crystals against metallic plates. Responds slightly faster than sand. Patterns form cleanly. The cubic crystal shape gives a slightly faceted appearance to nodal ridges.
limitations: Hygroscopic — absorbs moisture and clumps in humid conditions. Corrodes steel plates over time. Must be dried before use in humid environments.
preparation: Spread on paper and leave in warm oven 15 min to remove moisture. Use fine table salt — not coarse sea salt.
safety: Food safe. Non-hazardous. Avoid contact with steel plate surface if long-term use — use aluminum or glass plates.

name: Water (Pure / Distilled)
visual_effect: Faraday wave surface patterns — hexagonal cells, stripes, rings at half the driving frequency
best_for: Faraday wave demonstrations, organic flowing patterns, biological form visualization
behavior_description: Water cymatics operates via the Faraday instability — parametric excitation of surface waves at half the driving frequency. Above the threshold amplitude, standing surface wave patterns form spontaneously. The pattern type (hexagons, squares, stripes) depends on frequency and container geometry. Patterns are dynamic and three-dimensional.
limitations: Patterns appear at f/2, not f. Highly sensitive to surface contamination. Splashing at high amplitudes. Pattern changes rapidly with frequency — not as stable as dry methods.
preparation: Use distilled water for reproducibility. Wipe container clean of oils. Temperature affects surface tension — use consistent temperature.
safety: Non-hazardous. Electrical safety: keep electronics dry.
availability: Distilled water available at supermarkets and pharmacies

name: Cornstarch Suspension (Oobleck)
visual_effect: Dramatic standing fingers, writhing columns, self-organizing blobs — most spectacular visual effect in cymatics
best_for: Maximum visual drama, video and film, art installations, public engagement
behavior_description: Cornstarch in water (approximately 1.5-2 parts cornstarch by mass) forms a dilatant (shear-thickening) non-Newtonian fluid. Under high-frequency vibration it exhibits dramatic behaviors: upward-pointing fingers and tendrils that persist for seconds, pulsating blobs, and finger-printing patterns. Not classical nodal-line formation — much more visually dynamic.
limitations: Settles and separates on standing — must stir regularly. Difficult to clean. Patterns are chaotic and difficult to reproduce precisely. Cracks on surfaces. Not suitable for fine nodal line visualization.
preparation: Mix 1 part water with 1.5-2 parts cornstarch by mass. Add water slowly while stirring. Let stand 5 min. Consistency should flow slowly when tilted but feel solid when pressed quickly.
safety: Food-safe. Non-hazardous. Avoid breathing dust during preparation.

name: Iron Filings
visual_effect: Dark fuzzy nodal lines with directional texture — reveals field-line-like structure
best_for: Metallic aesthetic, demonstrations combining acoustics and magnetism, industrial-look photography
behavior_description: Iron filings behave similarly to sand in the Chladni context but produce darker, more textured lines. When a magnet is placed near the plate, filings can also be pre-oriented, creating combined acoustic-magnetic patterns. The fuzzy texture gives patterns an organic quality. Filings tend to clump more than sand.
limitations: High density requires more amplitude than sand. Will magnetize and stick to steel plates permanently — use non-magnetic aluminum or acrylic plates. Hard to clean completely.
preparation: Sieve to remove large clumps. Keep dry and away from magnets before use.
safety: Avoid inhalation. May rust with humidity. Keep away from electronics and magnets.
availability: Scientific supply companies, physics demo suppliers, craft stores

name: Graphite Powder
visual_effect: Fine dark gray lines with subtle metallic sheen — extremely precise nodal boundaries
best_for: High-frequency demonstrations, precision photography, dark contrasting backgrounds (use light-colored plates)
behavior_description: Very fine graphite powder provides high-resolution Chladni patterns. The dark color contrasts well with aluminum or glass plates. Fine grain size enables sensitivity near that of lycopodium but with a striking metallic-dark visual quality. Electrically conductive — nodal lines can be used as resistive traces in experimental setups.
limitations: Dark color makes patterns harder to see on steel plates. Can be messy — graphite stains. Fine particles become airborne easily.
preparation: Use technical-grade graphite powder (not pencil graphite). Keep dry in sealed container.
safety: Fine particle inhalation hazard — wear dust mask. Stains skin and clothing. Conduct electricity — avoid contact with energized circuits.
availability: Industrial lubricant suppliers, scientific supply stores, art supply stores

name: Wheat Flour (Fine)
visual_effect: Bright white powdery nodal lines — excellent contrast on dark or metallic plates
best_for: Food-safe demonstrations, classroom settings, quick accessible experiments, artistic use
behavior_description: Readily available food material that produces high-quality Chladni patterns. White flour is finer than salt and forms sharper lines than coarse sand. Slightly hygroscopic but tolerates moderate humidity. An excellent starting material for home experiments.
limitations: Absorbs moisture and clumps in humid conditions. Not as fine as lycopodium — limited to mid frequencies. Can become moldy if left on plate for extended periods.
preparation: Use all-purpose white flour, sifted. Do not use self-rising flour (contains leavening agents that may cake). Dry in oven at 80°C for 20 min if humid.
safety: Food-safe. Avoid inhalation of flour dust (flour dust can cause occupational asthma with repeated exposure).

name: Fine Sugar (Caster/Confectioner's)
visual_effect: Sparkling white nodal lines — sugar crystals catch light producing sparkle effects under studio lights
best_for: Artistic cymatics photography, sparkle-effect visualization, accessible home demonstrations
behavior_description: Caster or confectioner's sugar forms clean Chladni patterns with a distinctive sparkle when illuminated with raking or side-lit studio light. The transparent nature of sugar crystals allows interesting light diffraction effects. Behaves similarly to salt but produces slightly softer-edged patterns due to rounded crystal shape.
limitations: Dissolves in any moisture — extremely sensitive to humidity. Becomes sticky when moist. Attracts insects.
preparation: Use confectioner's (icing) sugar for finest particles. Dry thoroughly before use. Keep in sealed container.

name: Colored Sand (Mixed Colors)
color: Multiple colors available: red, blue, green, black, pink, purple, etc.
visual_effect: Vivid colored geometric patterns — allows multiple-frequency patterns to be color-coded
best_for: Art installations, multiple-frequency visualization (different colors for different passes), educational displays, commercial demonstrations
behavior_description: Dyed sand particles behave identically to natural sand but allow visual differentiation. Useful technique: apply red sand and excite at frequency A, photograph; add blue sand over same plate and excite at frequency B. Two patterns visible in different colors simultaneously. Used extensively in artistic cymatics photography.
limitations: Dye may fade with humidity. Color contamination between different color batches. Same frequency limitations as natural sand.
preparation: Commercial colored craft sand is ready to use. Ensure different colors are of similar grain size for comparable response.
safety: Non-hazardous. Dye may stain skin and clothing.
availability: Craft stores, art supply stores, party suppliers
